---
title: "Chapter 11 · Distributionally Robust Enterprise Optimization"
subtitle: "Volume II · Enterprise Optimization Under Uncertainty · Week 12"
---

::: {.chapter-meta}
**Volume II, Chapter 11** · Taught in [Week 12](../schedule.html#week-12) · Lab seed `26211`
:::

## Learning Outcome Statements

After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:

```{=html}
<ol class="los-list"><li>Distinguish stochastic, robust, neuro-fuzzy robust, and distributionally robust optimization.</li><li>Define ambiguity sets for enterprise uncertainty.</li><li>Formulate distributionally robust enterprise optimization problems.</li><li>Construct moment-based ambiguity sets.</li><li>Construct Wasserstein ambiguity sets.</li><li>Construct $\varphi$-divergence ambiguity sets.</li><li>Interpret worst-case probability distributions.</li><li>Analyze enterprise decisions under distributional ambiguity.</li><li>Implement DREO computationally with calibrated radii and honest certificates.</li><li>Prepare enterprise models for multi-objective optimization.</li></ol>
```

## Reading Guide

Work through the chapter in section order; the full development, proofs, and worked
examples are in the book — this page indexes them and does not replace them.

1. Motivation for Distributionally Robust Enterprise Optimization
2. Enterprise Distributional Ambiguity
3. Ambiguity Sets
4. Moment-Based Ambiguity Sets
5. Wasserstein Ambiguity Sets
6. \texorpdfstring{$\varphi$
7. Worst-Case Distribution Theory
8. Distributionally Robust Enterprise Policies
9. Computational Algorithms
10. Enterprise Applications
11. Comparison with NF-REO
12. Preparation for Multi-Objective Enterprise Optimization
13. Chapter Summary
14. Worked Examples
15. Exercises
16. Notes and Sources

## AXIOM Laboratory

This chapter is instrumented by: [AXIOM-11 →](https://axiom-webapp.example/modules/axiom-11)

Launch the module, load the chapter model, modify inputs, run the optimization,
and compare against the worked examples in the book.

## Exercises

12 exercises, grouped **A** concept checks · **B** mathematical ·
**C** computational · **D** enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the
advanced track. **Full solutions appear in the Instructor's Manual, Chapter 11.**

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part A — Concept checks</h4>

- **Exercise 11.1** — For each source row of Table (see book), justify the recommended set-design response and exhibit an enterprise decision where the *wrong* family (e.
- **Exercise 11.2** — Explain, using the saddle point of Theorem (see book), why publishing the worst-case distribution alongside a DR decision is the correct governance disclosure, and what a board should do when that law is (a) absurd, (b) plausible.

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part B — Mathematical exercises</h4>

- **Exercise 11.3** — Derive Scarf's bound of Example (see book) from the dual (see book) with $\psi = (\xi, \xi^{2})$: exhibit the optimal quadratic majorant of $(\xi - d)^{+}$ and the two-point worst-case law, verifying Theorem (see book)'s $k + 1 = 3$ bound is not tight here.
- **Exercise 11.4** — Prove the KL closed form (see book) on finite support: Lagrangian, exponential-tilt stationarity, and the one-dimensional dual; verify the Example (see book) weights.
- **Exercise 11.5** — Prove the $d = 1$ concentration inequality behind Proposition (see book) using $\Wass(P, Q) = \int\abs{F_P - F_Q}$ and Dvoretzky--Kiefer--Wolfowitz; calibrate $c_1, c_2$ explicitly for compactly supported laws.
- **Exercise 11.6** — Show the worst-case expectation of Definition (see book) is a coherent risk measure, and identify the ambiguity sets representing (a) CVaR$_{\alpha}$, (b) mean plus $\theta \times$ standard deviation (state precisely when the latter fails coherence).

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part C — Computational exercises</h4>

- **Exercise 11.7** — Prove strong duality in Theorem (see book) directly for finite $\Xi$ via LP duality, and exhibit a two-point $\Xi$ instance with the Slater condition violated where a duality gap opens.
- **Exercise 11.8** — Prove that for $\varepsilon > 0$ fixed and $N \to \infty$, the Wasserstein DREO decision converges to the $\varepsilon$-robust decision around $P_{\mathrm{true}}$, and combine with Proposition (see book) to obtain the full consistency diagram of data-driven DREO.

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part D — Enterprise applications</h4>

- **Exercise 11.9** — Reproduce Example (see book); then implement the Wasserstein-DR newsvendor on support $[0, \infty)$ via Theorem (see book)(ii) and report whether transport-based sets repair the disappointment gap at matched regret.
- **Exercise 11.10** — Build the cyber layer schedule of Example (see book) across retentions $d \in [3, 10]$ under (a) Scarf, (b) Scarf plus unimodality (Gauss-type bound; Notes), (c) fitted lognormal; plot the three curves and locate where structure assumptions matter most.
- **Exercise 11.11** — Meridian growth-market entry: quarterly demand history ($N = 16$, provided in the AXIOM-11 module) with suspected survivorship bias.
- **Exercise 11.12 ★** — Develop dynamic DREO for the Chapter 9 controlled diffusion: define rectangular Wasserstein ambiguity per time step, derive the distributionally robust HJB equation, prove a verification theorem, and quantify on the liquidity instance how the Chapter 9 gain stiffens with the per-step radius; discuss time-consistency failures for non-rectangular sets.

## Downloads

| Resource | File | Seed |
|---|---|---|
| Lecture deck | `DCT_V2_Ch11_Slides.pptx` *(forthcoming)* | — |
| Python notebook | `DCT_V2_Ch11_Lab.ipynb` *(forthcoming)* | `26211` |
| Excel workbook | `DCT_V2_Ch11_Lab.xlsx` *(forthcoming)* | `26211` |

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
All three companions consume the same seeded engine (`26211`), so their numbers
agree by construction — the MFMF convention, carried forward.
:::
